assertiveness_why: Prepared multi-sentence questions with good follow-ups on industry dynamics and business models, but mostly receptive. Harry is engaged but doesn't push back on claims.
question_style: Open-ended industry and business model questions; Harry builds follow-ups by asking about different aspects (market state, differentiation, pricing, barriers); no pushback or challenges; focuses on understanding and elaboration.
tone: Warm and collaborative; Harry opens with effusive praise ("never before embodied product founder fit"); friendly and interested; appreciative of insights without sycophancy.
depth: Medium-to-high; tracks multiple dimensions (market evolution, consumer vs. enterprise, business models, pricing, barriers, transformative technologies, M&A); asks about specific applications and industries; some technical depth on machine learning and FAA regulations.
harry_quotes: Can I ask, have we seen massive technological and mechanical advances in these four years?
How do you perceive the state of the commercial drone market at present?
What are the key transformative technologies that will enable new applications?
learnings: Commercial drone adoption depends on enterprises digitizing physical operations and decision-making, not on consumer toy markets maturing into enterprise use.

Enterprise software in emerging categories must follow existing enterprise playbooks (seat-based, usage-based pricing) while offering professional services to ensure business outcomes, not just technology licensing.
Solutions developed for one vertical market's data analysis often yield immediate applicability to other verticals, creating platform value from vertical-specific expertise.
notable: Jonathan articulates the market maturation from hardware-focused to software-focused companies with clarity unusual for May 2017. His insight on digitizing physical business operations as the real catalyst resonates throughout.
